concepts.title: TCP and UDP
concepts.description: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) is a connection-oriented protocol, ensuring reliable data transfer, whereas UDP (User Datagram Protocol) is a connectionless protocol, prioritizing speed over reliability.

concepts.title: OSI Model Layers
concepts.description: The OSI model consists of 7 layers: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, and Application.

concepts.title: Network Topologies
concepts.description: Common network topologies include Bus, Star, Ring, and Mesh. Bus topology is linear, Star topology has a central hub, Ring topology is circular, and Mesh topology has multiple connections between nodes.

concepts.title: OSI Model Layers Mnemonic
concepts.description: Painstakingly Dozens Never Stir Parents After Sunset (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application) helps remember the 7 layers of the OSI model.

concepts.title: TCP vs UDP
concepts.description: Students often confuse TCP and UDP, thinking they are interchangeable. However, TCP is used for applications requiring guaranteed delivery, such as email and file transfer, while UDP is used for real-time applications like video streaming.

concepts.title: IP Address Classes
concepts.description: The IP address classes are A, B, C, D, and E, with Class A having 8-16 bits, Class B having 16-24 bits, and Class C having 24-32 bits. Class D is for multicast addresses, and Class E is reserved.

concepts.title: Network Devices
concepts.description: Common network devices include Hubs, Switches, Routers, Modems, and Gateways. Hubs are basic repeaters, Switches are intelligent devices, Routers connect networks, Modems connect to the internet, and Gateways act as translators.

concepts.title: HTTP vs FTP
concepts.description: HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is used for web browsing, while FTP (File Transfer Protocol) is used for file transfer. HTTP uses TCP, whereas FTP uses both TCP and UDP.
